title: Site Reliability Engineering
subtitle: How Google Runs Production Systems

editor.name: Betsy Beyer, Chris Jones, Jennifer Petoff, Niall Richard Murphy

contributors: Ben Treynor Sloss (Google VP, 24/7 Operations, SRE founder)
themes: Operations as a software engineering problem — applying engineering rigor to operational work
Error budgets resolve the structural conflict between feature velocity and reliability
SLOs, SLIs, and SLAs as the foundation for data-driven reliability decisions
Eliminating toil through automation to free SRE time for engineering work
Blameless postmortems create a culture of learning rather than blame
Simplicity is a core reliability strategy — every line of code is a liability
The four golden signals: latency, traffic, errors, saturation
Distributed consensus and load balancing at unprecedented scale
excerpt: The overwhelming majority of a distributed system's lifespan is spent in use, not in design or implementation. The discipline of site reliability engineering — treating operations as a software engineering problem — is how Google keeps its massive production systems running reliably while releasing changes at breakneck speed. This book is the inside story of that discipline.
